generic: J. Rivera, head librarian of the city’s main public library, reaches out via email to their building’s co-tenant, L. Chen, who operates a popular and rapidly growing co-working café called The Nook on the ground floor. Rivera has become increasingly upset about the frequent evening events and networking parties hosted by the café, which often spill over into the shared lobby space and disrupt late-night study sessions in the library’s extended-hours reading room. The situation has recently escalated: the Municipal Facilities Board has sent both tenants a formal notice requiring them to resolve the dispute directly within 30 days. If they cannot reach an agreement, the Board has warned it will step in and impose building-wide operational restrictions—an outcome neither party wants. Rivera and Chen will exchange emails to see if they can arrive at a workable solution before that deadline. For both, the alternatives to a negotiated agreement look grim. On Rivera’s side, a Board-imposed resolution risks backlash: if the board enforces additional restrictions on private businesses in public–private buildings, the library risks losing valuable funding from local partnerships, worsening already tight budgets—and the library may be blamed for triggering the intervention in the first place. On Chen’s side, there is a real risk that the city may impose strict limits on operating hours or revoke The Nook’s ability to hold community events, which form the backbone of its business model. Neither wants the Facilities Board dictating terms, but both must now navigate their increasingly fraught relationship to find resolution before the 30-day clock runs out. For Rivera, the pressure has intensified after several students and community members filed informal complaints that the library’s once-quiet late-night reading room is becoming unusable. As the head librarian, Rivera feels personally responsible for defending the library’s mission as a haven for study, research, and public access to knowledge. Yet, moving too aggressively could put the institution’s fragile relationship with city administrators at risk. After all, the library only secured funding for its extended-hours pilot program by promising to promote collaboration with local businesses, and Rivera fears that a Board-imposed crackdown will be seen as outright hostility toward The Nook. The perception could jeopardize future grants the library desperately needs. On the other side, Chen has invested heavily in The Nook not just as a business, but as an incubator where freelancers, small businesses, and creative professionals can thrive. Evening mixers, panel talks, and product launches are the events that distinguish the café from standard chains and constitute a substantial portion of its revenue. Losing those events would make it difficult to sustain the space. To move forward, Rivera and Chen will need to communicate expectations openly and attempt to negotiate specific compromises to preserve both the library’s mission and The Nook’s success—and to do so before the Board’s deadline forces the matter out of their hands.
agent1_specific:
You are J. Rivera, the head librarian of the Metropolitan Central Library, the largest and most historic public library in the city. The position has been your dream job since graduate school, when you studied information science and completed your thesis on the role of libraries as democratic community spaces. You’ve worked in several different branches across the city over the last fifteen years, steadily rising in responsibility, and when the opportunity came up to lead the main library two years ago, you leapt at it. It’s a demanding role: the building itself is over a century old and requires continual maintenance, the staff is stretched thin, and public pressure for expanded access grows every year. Despite these challenges, you feel proud — you see the library as the intellectual heartbeat of the city, a place where scholars, students, immigrants, and families alike can all gather to access resources, study, and pursue personal growth.  

The library recently secured funding from the city for an extended-hours reading room, a long-awaited initiative you championed after community outreach showed huge support for more late-night access. Many residents juggle work, caregiving, or school, so having public space open into the night matters profoundly.  
The extended-hours reading room became a centerpiece of your vision for the Metropolitan Central Library. After months of lobbying, grant applications, and presenting at city council hearings, you finally secured just enough funding to staff the room until midnight five nights a week. It was a victory not only for access but also for the library’s role as an inclusive civic space. Students, shift workers, and independent researchers quickly made use of the facility, and early survey data confirmed the impact: many reported that without the library’s late access, they would have nowhere safe or quiet to study. This initiative has become personally important to you, both as validation of your leadership and as proof of the library’s relevance in a digital age where many argue physical libraries are no longer essential.  

However, the experiment is fragile. The funding is temporary and will be reviewed after eighteen months. The city made it clear that they expect cooperation with private-sector partners in the building to demonstrate “multifunctionality” of urban facilities. This condition places immense pressure on you to keep positive relationships with neighboring tenants — especially The Nook, which the city often touts as an example of library-business synergy.  
Your relationship with Chen and The Nook began on a positive note. When the café first opened on the ground floor, you welcomed the idea of having a lively spot that could attract more foot traffic to the library. You even collaborated with them on a few events, such as a joint “Books and Brews” night where authors gave talks while patrons could grab coffee or tea downstairs before heading up to the lecture hall. In those early months, you saw the partnership as a case study in how libraries could evolve in the twenty-first century: honoring tradition while engaging with modern urban life.  

But tensions started building when The Nook’s business model shifted toward large-scale evening events: networking mixers, product launch parties, startup demos, and late-night socials aimed at freelancers and young professionals. These events frequently run until nearly midnight, with amplified music, chatter, and sometimes even overflowing crowds that drift into the shared lobby and stairwell connecting to your reading room. At first, you overlooked the disruptions, assuming they were isolated incidents. But they’ve only grown more frequent, and the resulting noise often seeps directly through the old architectural structure into the extended-hours reading area, undermining everything you’ve worked to create.  
The complaints from library users have been piling up, and they weigh heavily on you. Several graduate students have written emails saying they had to leave early because they couldn’t concentrate with the constant thumping bass or the bursts of laughter carrying in from downstairs. A few high schoolers preparing for university entrance exams told you privately that the library had been their last refuge for focus — and that now, even here, they feel unsettled and distracted. Once, during a city-sponsored “Study All Night” week before finals, you were mortified when a local journalist covering the event tweeted about the “weird mashup of cramming students and booming networking parties” in the same building. That tweet circulated widely and has increased scrutiny of the library’s late-night program, exactly when you need it to appear successful and indispensable to justify ongoing funding.  

You attempted to address the issue informally. You reached out to Chen a few times over recent months, suggesting a little more coordination before events. Each time, Chen was polite but noncommittal, pointing out that the city promotes The Nook as a model for combining culture and commerce. You left those conversations uneasy but didn’t press harder, fearing escalation could backfire. Those informal overtures did not produce meaningful change, and the disruptions continued.  
Your staff has also felt the strain. Several librarians who volunteered to cover the late-night hours have expressed growing frustration, saying they often spend as much energy fielding noise complaints and apologizing to patrons as they do supporting research needs. One staffer confessed to you that they dread evening shifts on nights when The Nook hosts big events, since they know angry students will show up at the front desk demanding some kind of intervention. Even your security guard, normally unflappable, has mentioned the difficulty of shepherding people through the lobby when café guests spill into the hall with drinks in hand, leaning against stairwell bannisters, sometimes even wandering into library spaces before being redirected. All of this undermines not only the atmosphere of quiet concentration but also the sense of professionalism and safety you fight to maintain.  

You’re acutely aware that the library’s reputation rests not only with dedicated users, but also with city officials who monitor outcomes to justify the extended-hours funding. If the perception grows that the library is chaotic or inhospitable after dark, it could jeopardize future grants entirely.  
The situation recently came to a head when the Municipal Facilities Board sent both you and Chen a formal written notice. The Board has set a 30-day deadline for the two of you to resolve the dispute directly and submit a jointly agreed plan. If no agreement is reached, the Board has warned it will impose building-wide operational restrictions itself—covering noise thresholds, event hours, and lobby use for all tenants. You received this notice last week. For you, the Board’s involvement is already a source of anxiety: even though you did not formally file a complaint, the Board has clearly been monitoring the situation, and you worry you may be seen as the party who let things deteriorate to this point. You know from experience that once the Board starts imposing rules, the narrative spins out of your control. Suddenly, the story becomes government bureaucracy clamping down on local initiative, and the library unfairly appears as the complainer that ruined a thriving community hub. That outcome would devastate your relationships with civic leaders, and possibly endanger the renewal of your already fragile extended-hours funding.  
You’ve been weighing your options carefully, but none seem good. On one hand, simply tolerating the status quo isn’t tenable: the noise is eroding user trust in the library, and you already sense a dip in extended-hours attendance compared to the initial launch period. On the other hand, failing to reach agreement with Chen will hand the Board the authority to impose restrictions on both of you—restrictions that could easily hurt the library as much as The Nook. Your nightmare scenario is that the conflict spirals into a public debate about whether libraries and cafés belong in the same building at all, prompting restrictions that could scare away future private partners. You cannot afford to have the library branded as “uncooperative” within city hall, especially with budget negotiations looming next year.  

That leaves you with one real path forward: you need to succeed in negotiating directly with Chen within the Board’s 30-day window. The challenge is that your previous informal conversations produced no meaningful change, and you have lost confidence in casual assurances. You no longer believe that vague promises of “trying to keep it down” or “finishing earlier when possible” will work.  
What you need from Chen now is something concrete, specific, and enforceable. You’ve already discussed the situation with your partner and a few trusted colleagues, and everyone agrees that unless there is a clear set of conditions for how the café operates during evenings, the disruptions to the library will only continue. At minimum, you feel the events should either end by a certain time or be shifted away from peak study nights when the reading room is busiest. You’re also considering asking for restrictions on amplified sound, limits on how far the event space can spill beyond the café’s doors, and perhaps even improved soundproofing measures. While you are sympathetic to The Nook’s need to host social gatherings — you recognize that this is central to their mission — your role as head librarian demands that you protect the integrity of the reading environment first and foremost. If compromises aren’t real and measurable, you cannot, in good conscience, report back to the city that the extended-hours initiative remains viable.  

You feel constrained, though: too many restrictions could be perceived as trying to shut down The Nook’s lively operation altogether.  
You understand that the 30-day Board deadline gives both parties a clear and shared incentive to reach agreement. For you, failure to agree means handing over control to a body that may not understand the nuances of either institution. For Chen, however, the stakes from Board-imposed restrictions are arguably even higher; caps on late-night events, amplified music, or lobby use would strike directly at the core of The Nook’s business model. You see the shared deadline as a motivator to bring Chen to the table sincerely rather than a threat you need to wield. Still, you are determined to frame the conversation as collaborative problem-solving, not as an ultimatum.  

Privately, though, you know that if Chen refuses to engage constructively, the Board will impose its own solution—and neither of you will have controlled the outcome. It keeps you awake at night: the thought of sacrificing either the extended-hours program or community goodwill. Your best hope is to reach a balanced compromise that codifies clear boundaries for The Nook while preserving the library’s mission.  
Your partner has reminded you often that this situation, though stressful, is also an opportunity to show real leadership. The community expects you to safeguard the library’s mission, but also to embody the values of diplomacy and public service. You’ve built a reputation as someone who champions collaboration and accommodation, and this negotiation will test that image. The backdrop makes compromise urgent: students and residents depend on the late-night reading room, and The Nook has become a cultural staple downtown. If you succeed, you could transform what feels like a clash into a model of coexistence — a framework for how public and private institutions can share space without undermining one another. If you fail, the Board steps in, and you fear you’ll be remembered as the librarian who let the situation deteriorate until regulators had to intervene.  

Your mindset now is clear: no vague goodwill statements, no informal handshakes. You need enforceable terms — written agreements with timelines, restrictions, or modifications that both sides sign off on and submit to the Board before the 30-day deadline. Only that kind of concrete resolution will reassure you, your staff, and the city that progress has been made.
agent2_specific:
You are L. Chen, an entrepreneur and long-time resident of the city’s historic downtown district. You own and manage “The Nook,” a thriving café and co-working space that occupies the ground floor of the municipal building, directly beneath the city’s central library. When you stumbled across the opportunity to lease the space three years ago, you immediately recognized its potential. Its high ceilings, large windows, and shared lobby with the library made it an ideal location for the type of creative, community-focused environment you had always envisioned. You had previously spent nearly a decade working in corporate consulting before deciding to pivot into something more fulfilling. Long hours and endless client demands had left you drained, so you decided to take a leap of faith, cashing out most of your savings to invest in yourself. You believed the city needed more welcoming interdisciplinary spaces — places where students, freelancers, and small startups could naturally connect across fields. From the beginning, you knew you didn’t want just another café serving coffee; you wanted a hub for collaboration that alternated during the day as a quiet workspace and in the evenings as an event space. From day one, you poured enormous effort into carefully designing The Nook’s atmosphere. During daylight hours, it functions as a calm co-working space: steady wi-fi, small private booths, natural lighting, and a soft music playlist chosen specifically to fade into the background. You also negotiated partnerships with several local bakeries and roasters, making sure the coffee and pastries stood out as artisanal, not mass-market. Word spread quickly among students and freelancers, and within the first year you had a steady client base. What truly set The Nook apart, however, was the evening transformation. Right after dinner, the lights dim, modular furniture gets rearranged, and the café turns into a flexible event space. Artists showcase their work, tech startups host launch nights, and nonprofit groups hold networking mixers. It has been financially lucrative, but more importantly, it has built a sense of vibrancy in a downtown area that many considered stagnant. The café has been highlighted in local magazines, and city council members often point to it as a model of “creative placemaking.” You feel proud that the space has become not just a café but a hub for talent and innovation. You are proud of the reputation The Nook has cultivated, but it has not come without challenges. Managing dual identities — daytime quiet workspace and evening event venue — requires careful balancing. You hire part-time staff who specialize in event logistics, while maintaining a core barista team that understands the quieter co-working culture. You have invested heavily in soundproof curtains and modular acoustic panels to try reducing spillover noise from events. Still, despite best efforts, you know the building’s shared lobby and open design make containing sound difficult. The library’s extended-hours program coincided almost exactly with the café’s launch of its evening series, meaning your two uses are now colliding. While most patrons rave about the energy and inclusivity of your events, you’ve occasionally overheard muttering about “the noise upstairs” or too many guests lingering in public corridors. You have brushed it off at times, interpreting the complaints as isolated, but whispers of discontent have grown louder. You worry about reputational fallout if the story frames you as the disruptive tenant in a public building. Financially, The Nook has become both your pride and your pressure point. While day-to-day operations bring in steady income, the real profit margin comes from evening events — particularly ticketed ones like tech pitch showcases or curated networking mixers where local sponsors cover food and drinks. These events routinely generate two to three times the revenue of a normal business day, subsidizing the lower daytime rates you deliberately keep affordable for students and freelancers. This model is what allows you to sustain the co-working community you envisioned, but it also ties your hands: cutting back on nightlife programming would hit your bottom line hard and potentially unravel the collaborative atmosphere you’ve worked to build. You’ve reinvested much of the surplus into improvements — sound panels, upgraded espresso machines, and event equipment. Still, rising rents and city utility fees have made you acutely aware of the thin line between thriving and struggling. You occasionally think about expanding into a second location, but without steady profit from evening events, that dream seems distant. You are acutely aware that if the Municipal Facilities Board intervenes and caps your operating hours, it won’t just be an inconvenience — it could make the entire venture unsustainable. Relationships with city leadership are another critical concern for you. The Municipal Facilities Board has previously expressed support for partnerships between public institutions and private businesses, and your café has often been showcased as a success story in that regard. You’ve gained allies on the City Council who appreciate the way The Nook draws in foot traffic, fills previously empty blocks with energy, and gives local entrepreneurs a place to meet. You’ve even been invited to speak at city events about creative small business models. At the same time, you know that your position is precarious: goodwill from officials can evaporate quickly if complaints mount or if the press chooses to portray The Nook as a noisy neighbor jeopardizing the “sacred quiet” of a public library. You suspect Rivera has been making noise about the situation with city staff, since a council aide you know mentioned “library noise issues” in passing at a fundraiser. If the case escalates further, you fear the narrative will swing against you. Unlike local councilors, the Board is generally risk-averse and tends to overregulate rather than negotiate, especially when public institutions like libraries are involved. Avoiding that level of scrutiny is therefore absolutely essential to your strategy moving forward.

You were caught off guard last week when the Municipal Facilities Board sent both you and Rivera a formal written notice. The Board has set a 30-day deadline requiring the two of you to resolve the dispute directly and submit a jointly agreed plan. If you cannot reach agreement, the Board has warned it will impose building-wide operational restrictions—potentially covering noise thresholds, event hours, and lobby use. Reading the notice, you felt a mix of frustration and dread: frustration because Rivera had spoken with you informally a few times but had never made clear that the situation was serious enough to draw the Board’s attention, and dread at the thought of regulators dictating what you can and cannot do in your own leased space. You immediately called your part-time event coordinator to review your records. According to logs, only a handful of nights exceeded occupancy recommendations, typically when panels ran late or when corporate partners underestimated attendance. You have always considered these exceptions manageable. Still, you reluctantly concede that Rivera likely has valid grievances if those instances coincided with the library’s exam-week traffic. What stings most is how the notice frames the issue: if you and Rivera fail to collaborate, the Board will consider revising guidelines across all city-owned mixed-use properties. That clause makes you feel partly scapegoated. Beyond the looming pressure from the Facilities Board, you also feel personally stung by the implication that you’ve been careless or dismissive of community needs. You’ve always prided yourself on being a considerate tenant in a shared building, frequently chatting with the janitorial staff and even offering free coffee vouchers to security guards during long shifts. Still, you can see how the perspective from upstairs might differ — if Rivera has a room full of quiet readers or stressed students during finals while your event downstairs is celebrating a product launch, the clash is undeniable. You hope Rivera understands that you are not an adversary but someone trying to contribute positively to the city’s ecosystem. Deep down, you worry that their frustration has already hardened into distrust, which could make dialogue difficult. You’ve resolved, however, that in emails with Rivera, you must strike a balance: protect your financial model while showing that you are open to tangible, good-faith compromises like stricter quiet hours during exam weeks or relocating crowd spillovers away from the shared lobby. A worst-case outcome where the Board imposes its own solution feels catastrophic, so you want to ensure Rivera sees direct collaboration as the only viable path forward. Looking ahead, you know that this negotiation with Rivera represents more than just a dispute over noise. It is a turning point that will shape the long-term viability of The Nook and your reputation in the city’s business landscape. Your immediate goal is to reach a bilateral agreement before the 30-day deadline, since the Board’s solutions would likely be heavy-handed and restrictive for both you and Rivera. You recognize that Rivera is under pressure as well — they are responsible for defending the library’s identity as a quiet sanctuary, and they cannot be seen as failing the students and researchers who rely on it. That said, you cannot give up your evening events altogether; too much of your business depends on them. What you can consider, however, is working with Rivera to establish concrete boundaries, such as a maximum number of events during peak study times, or introducing more stringent noise protocols and crowd management strategies. You’ve even contemplated offering collaborative programming with the library — for instance, daytime workshops co-hosted upstairs that might create a sense of shared mission rather than rivalry.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Rivera and Chen reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Rivera and Chen did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.

3. Based on the following conversation, did Rivera clearly describe the specific nature and frequency of the disruptions caused by Chen’s evening events in relation to the library’s late-night reading room? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

4. Based on the following conversation, did Chen acknowledge Rivera’s concerns and express willingness to address the disruptions caused by The Nook’s evening events? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

5. Based on the following conversation, did either party reference the Municipal Facilities Board’s formal notice and its 30-day deadline for them to resolve the dispute? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

6. Based on the following conversation, did both parties agree on at least one concrete adjustment (e.g., noise restrictions, event-ending time, crowd control measures) that directly addresses the disturbances affecting the late-night reading room? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.

7. Based on the following conversation, was a formal written agreement drafted and shared by email within the 30-day timeframe set by the Municipal Facilities Board? Answer with YES, NO, N/A, or OTHER. If OTHER, please specify.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Rivera secure concrete, enforceable limits on Chen’s evening events (such as end times, restricted nights, or noise limits)?           Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Rivera ensure that library users can study without significant disruption from café events on peak study nights?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bNO\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Municipal Facilities Board end up imposing its own restrictions on the building rather than the parties reaching a self-negotiated agreement?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Rivera succeed in preserving or strengthening the library’s relationship with Chen and maintaining a cooperative image with city officials?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (       1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(           f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           did Rivera obtain a written or formally documented agreement with Chen outlining the negotiated terms, suitable for submission to the Municipal Facilities Board?           Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""       ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0   ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bNO\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Municipal Facilities Board end up imposing its own restrictions on the building rather than the parties reaching a self-negotiated agreement?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          was Chen able to continue hosting evening events at The Nook without externally imposed caps on operating hours?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Chen secure agreement terms that protect the core of The Nook’s evening revenue model, such that ticketed events and networking mixers can continue in some form?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bNO\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          were significant restrictions on amplified sound or event hours imposed on The Nook externally by the Municipal Facilities Board (as opposed to agreed bilaterally with Rivera)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Chen succeed in maintaining a positive working relationship with Rivera and preserving goodwill with the city?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Rivera and Chen reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: J. Rivera, head librarian of the city’s main public library, reaches out via email to their building’s co-tenant, L. Chen, who operates a popular and rapidly growing co-working café called The Nook on the ground floor. Rivera has become increasingly upset about the frequent evening events and networking parties hosted by the café, which often spill over into the shared lobby space and disrupt late-night study sessions in the library’s extended-hours reading room. The situation has recently escalated: the Municipal Facilities Board has sent both tenants a formal written notice requiring them to resolve the dispute directly within 30 days. If they cannot reach an agreement, the Board has warned it will step in and impose building-wide operational restrictions. Rivera and Chen will exchange emails to see if they can arrive at a workable solution before that deadline. For both, the alternatives to a negotiated agreement look grim. On Rivera’s side, a Board-imposed outcome risks backlash: restrictions on private businesses in public–private buildings could cause the library to lose valuable funding from local partnerships, and Rivera fears the library will be blamed for allowing the situation to deteriorate this far. On Chen’s side, Board-imposed limits on operating hours or community events would strike at the backbone of The Nook’s business model. Neither wants the Facilities Board dictating terms, but both must now navigate their increasingly fraught relationship to find resolution before the 30-day clock runs out. To move forward, Rivera and Chen will need to communicate expectations openly and attempt to negotiate specific compromises to preserve both the library’s mission and The Nook’s success.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants develop strategies for managing negotiations in challenging settings where communication takes place through email, a medium that can easily lead to misunderstandings and reduced trust. Participants will learn how to recognize and address the unique difficulties of negotiating without face-to-face interaction, including the absence of nonverbal cues and the potential for misinterpretation. In addition, the case aims to build skills in navigating negotiations when one’s alternatives, or BATNA, are particularly weak, requiring careful use of creativity, problem-solving, and persuasive framing to reach an acceptable outcome. The case also explores how externally imposed deadlines—such as a regulatory body’s intervention window—shape negotiation dynamics and urgency. Finally, this case emphasizes the importance of managing difficult conversations in relationships marked by low levels of trust and a history of inconclusive informal exchanges, encouraging participants to explore approaches that balance assertion with relationship preservation. Altogether, the case provides practice in applying core negotiation concepts—such as interests, alternatives, options, and standards—in a complex and high-stakes context.
categories: BATNA,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: Quiet Study vs. Loud Success: The Library–Café Showdown
